mnemonics.title: Stars and Bars Count
mnemonics.content: Remember: To count stars and bars, think of 'r stars need r-1 bars' - WRONG! Actually n-1 bars for n categories. Mnemonic: 'Bars divide Categories: Count Categories, subtract ONE'.

mnemonics.title: Formula Memory
mnemonics.content: C(n+r-1, r) - Think 'n Plus r Minus ONE, choose r' - The '-1' is the key twist in combinations with repetition!

mnemonics.title: When to Use
mnemonics.content: CRR: Combinations with Repetition when - 'C'hoosing, 'R'epetition allowed, 'R'andom order (doesn't matter). NOT for permutations or no-repetition cases.

acronyms.usage: Use when selecting items where Order doesn't matter and Repetition is allowed
Visual method using stars (*) for items and bars (|) as dividers between categories

acronyms.expansion: Binomial coefficient for combinations with repetition
acronyms.usage: Formula: Choose r positions from (n+r-1) for either stars or bars

visualTips.visualization: Draw r stars and n-1 bars in a row. Example for 3 types, 5 items: **|*|**. This means 2 of type1, 1 of type2, 2 of type3. Total objects = r + (n-1) = 5 + 2 = 7 positions.
Think of placing r identical balls into n distinct boxes. You need n-1 dividers (bars) to separate boxes. Total positions = r + (n-1). Choose positions for the r stars: C(r+n-1, r).
Regular C(n,r): select r from n without repeats. With repetition C(n+r-1, r): it's like having r+n-1 items and choosing r, giving more possibilities due to repetition.

analogies.analogy: Like a grocery store where you can buy multiple of the same fruit - you're just counting how many of each type, not the order of picking.
Giving out identical cookies to children - only matters how many cookies each child gets, not which specific cookie (since they're identical).
Dropping letters into mailboxes - only the count per mailbox matters, not which specific letter goes where (if letters identical).
